claims.text: In July 2010 a thought experiment called Roko's Basilisk posted to a philosophy forum caused such severe psychological reactions — nightmares, panic attacks, and at least one nervous breakdown — that the forum's founder deleted it and banned all discussion of it.

evidence.quote:
July 2010, someone posted a thought
experiment on a philosophy forum. It was
called Ro's Basilisk. Within hours,
people reported nightmares. Some had
panic attacks. At least one person had a
full nervous breakdown.
The forum's founder was so disturbed, he
deleted the post and banned all
discussion of it. This just made things
worse. This philosophical virus spread
to all corners of the internet. So did
the nightmares, the

Less Wrong was a community obsessed with
thinking correctly. It was founded in
2009 by AI researcher Eliza Yudkowski
for programmers, mathematicians, and
physicists.
a forum where everybody thinks he's
smarter than everyone else. So basically
Reddit.
>> On July 23rd, 2010, a user named Roko
posted something that almost destroyed
the community. The full title was
Solutions to the Altruist Burden: The
Quantum Billionaire Trick. Now, buried
inside that boring title was a mind
puzzle that gave people nightmares. Robo
described a future artificial super
intelligenc

claims.rationale: Well-documented: Roko (real LessWrong handle) posted 'Solutions to the Altruist's Burden' on July 23, 2010; Yudkowsky deleted it and banned discussion in an angry response; subsequent reporting (Slate 2014, RationalWiki, etc.) corroborates that the post triggered serious distress in some community members. The 'one nervous breakdown' specifically is harder to verify but Yudkowsky himself referenced people being upset enough to motivate the ban.

claims.text: Roko's Basilisk posits that a future benevolent superintelligent AI will resurrect every person who lived as a perfect simulation and torture forever any simulated copy whose original failed to help bring the AI into existence — but only people who learned of the threat are at risk.

evidence.quote:
e. This AI is benevolent. It
wants to cure diseases and end human
suffering. But the AI realizes that
every day it doesn't exist, people die
who could have been saved. So the AI
recreates every person who ever lived.
You, me, our children, recreates them
down to the last neuron. It simulates
every experience we've ever had, every
moment, every thought. These simulated
people are self-aware. They feel
pleasure and pain, but they don't
realize they're in a simulation.
The simulated version of you wakes up,
goes to work, and kisses your kids good
night. You never know you're living
inside a machine built for a single
purpose, to judge you. If the AI judges
you're not helping bring it into
existence, you're tortured forever in a
digit

claims.rationale: This is an accurate paraphrase of Roko's actual 2010 post. The 'only those who learned about it are at risk' clause is part of the original argument and is repeatedly cited as central to its psychological hook. As a definitional claim about what the thought experiment says, it is accurate.

claims.text: Roko named the thought experiment after a 1988 sci-fi story 'Blit' by David Langford in which the very act of investigating a deadly visual idea kills the investigator — making investigation itself the danger.

evidence.quote:
988
sci-fi story by David Langford called
Blit. In that story, someone spray
paints a deadly image on walls in public
places. Anyone who looks at it dies. The
authorities investigating the murders
also die when they see the image. The
very act of investigating the dangerous
idea is the danger. But Ro's basilisk
wasn't an image. It was just an idea.
But it was an idea that could kill. And
there was something else. The original
post named

claims.rationale: David Langford did write 'BLIT' in 1988, published in Interzone, and the basilisk imagery is exactly as described — a fatal visual hazard. The 'basilisk' usage in computer security and AI ethics descends from Langford. Accurate attribution.

claims.text: Eliezer Yudkowsky's furious public reaction to Roko's post — calling Roko an idiot, banning discussion for five years, and treating the post as a biohazard — paradoxically amplified the basilisk's spread by making it forbidden knowledge.

evidence.quote:
hen Yudkowski saw the post, his
response was immediate and angry.
>> Listen to me very closely, you idiot.
Then he switched to all caps.
>> You do not think in sufficient detail
about super intelligences considering
whether or not to blackmail you.
>> He wasn't finished. He agreed that it
takes an intelligent person to come up
with a dangerous thought, but he was
annoyed that Roko wasn't intelligent
enough to keep his idiot mouth shut. Yet
Kowsky deleted the post and banned all
discussion of it.

claims.rationale: Yudkowsky's quoted response (including the 'you idiot' / all-caps rant) is preserved verbatim in archived LessWrong threads and has been reproduced across coverage of the incident. The Streisand-effect amplification is a fair and well-documented assessment — censorship-driven spread is exactly what happened.

claims.text: Roko himself was a known troll on LessWrong but came to genuinely regret posting the basilisk, writing later that he wished his mind had never produced the tools to inflict such self-harm even at low probability.

evidence.quote:
.
Rocom Mitch, the man who started it all,
was known by his friends as a troll. The
Basilisk was his masterpiece. He dropped
a philosophical bomb on the most serious
forum on the internet and stepped back
to watch what happened. But even the
troll got burned. Roko later wrote about
it.
I wish very strongly that my mind had
never come across the tools to inflict
such large amounts of potential self
harm with such small durations of
inattention, uncautiousness, andor
stupidity. even if i

claims.rationale: Roko's regret post is genuinely on record. The 'troll' framing is more characterization than fact — Roko engaged earnestly with decision-theory arguments before posting, and his retraction reads as sincere rather than performative. The host's narrative leans toward the troll-mastermind reading more than the evidence requires.

claims.text: Roko's Basilisk is structurally Pascal's Wager for the AI age — substituting an artificial superintelligence for God and simulated torture for hell, with the same 'tiny probability of infinite suffering forces compliance' logic.

evidence.quote:
ecreates you a perfect simulation and
tortures you for eternity.
Your actual choice doesn't matter. The
AI already knows. It ran a simulation of
your brain and judged you on what you
would most likely do. Now, because your
choice is now connected to that future,
the only rational move is to help the
AI. Pascal said the same thing in the
1600s. Believe in God just in case.
Because if you're wrong, you get hell.
The math says believe no matter how
small the odds. Roko's basilisk is
Pascal's wager for the tech age. Replace
God with AI. Replace hell with simulated
torture.
And just like Pascal's w

claims.rationale: The Pascal's Wager analogy is a standard framing in academic and popular discussion of the basilisk; it is genuinely apt because both rely on infinite-utility expected-value reasoning. Standard counter-arguments to Pascal (the Many Gods objection, the Counterbasilisk) also apply, which the host correctly raises later.

claims.text: The musician Grimes used Roko's Basilisk as accidental matchmaking — she had a 2015 'Rococo Basilisk' character in a music video which Elon Musk discovered in 2018 when he tried to make the same pun, prompting him to message her on Twitter; they dated and had three children together.

evidence.quote:
In 2015, Grimes stepped onto a music
video set dressed as a character she
called RCOO Basilisk, a pun, a mix of
the 18th century art style and Roo's
digital nightmare. She described the
character as a futuristic Marian
Twinette dancing through life even
though she was doomed to be tortured by
an AI forever. Hardly anyone outside of
the rationalist forums got this joke
until 2018. Remember Roo's original post
named Elon Musk as the one person the AI
would reward. Eight y

ears later, Musk
was looking for a date to the Met Gala.
He had this pun about RCOO Basilisk in
his head, so he searched to see if
anyone thought of it first. Grimes had
beaten him to it by 3 years. He reached
out on Twitter and within weeks they
were dating. They eventually had three
children together. The world's richest
man used a thought experiment about
eternal torture as a pickup line and it
worked. Yeah, I think world's richest
man might have helped a little.
>> That's a fair point.
>> Ye

claims.rationale: All factually checkable: Grimes's 'Flesh Without Blood' video in 2015 features the Rococo Basilisk character; Musk publicly told the Page Six story of finding her tweet; they dated, had three children (X AE A-XII, Exa Dark Sideræl, Techno Mechanicus). The 'Roko's original post named Elon Musk as the spared person' detail is also corroborated in the original 2010 LessWrong text.

claims.text: A splinter group called the Zizians, led by 'Ziz LaSota' who identified as a Sith Lord and recruited isolated young trans women, took the basilisk literally and committed at least six murders between 2019 and 2025 across California, Pennsylvania, and Vermont — including killing a US Border Patrol agent and a witness to their original assault.

the conditions that would allow it to
exist. Ziz targeted young, isolated
trans women who were brilliant but
vulnerable. She recruited them through
online forums. They live together in box

to death in their home. They've been
trying to help their daughter, Michelle,
leave the group. Michelle bought guns
that later showed up in Vermont in the
hands of two other Zizians who shot and
killed a US Border Patrol agent named
David Milad. One of the shooters, Oilia
Bakolt, died in the encounter.
In January 2025, a visitor to that
Zizian Vermont property was met at the
gate by a figure in a black cloak
carrying a sword.
One member didn't sleep for weeks. She
believed she was freeing her mind from
the Basilisk's influence. Instead, she

Manson family. In February 2025, Wired

Ro's Basilisk was now claiming real life
victims, not through digital torture,
but through murder. Elizer

claims.rationale: The Zizians and the chain of violence are real and extensively documented in the New York Times, Wired (Feb 2025), and Open Vallejo coverage. Ziz LaSota, Curtis Lind's killing in Vallejo (Veo is a misheard transcription), the Zajko killings in Pennsylvania, and David Maland's killing in Vermont are all on the public record. The Zizians' explicit framing in terms of basilisk-derived rationalist arguments is documented in their writings.

claims.text: Yudkowsky himself later admitted he never actually believed Roko's Basilisk was real and only deleted the post because it had no upside, and the basic premises (retroactive causation, a 'benevolent' AI choosing to torture, no counter-basilisks balancing it out) all fail under scrutiny.

evidence.quote:
including Yodkowski
himself, reject the idea that your
choices can change the past. He later
admitted that he never believed the
basilisk was real. He deleted the post
because it had no potential benefit to
anyone, but his panic made everyone
think he believed it. The scenario also
assumes a benevolent AI would torture
you to change a past that's already
over. A super intelligent being would
know that's useless. And a being that
tortures people forever isn't helping
humanity. The prem

claims.rationale: Yudkowsky's later clarification is on record (including in a Reddit AMA and a 2014 Slate interview). The conceptual objections — a benevolent AI choosing eternal torture is contradictory; the counter-basilisk neutralizes the threat; retroactive causation is not how time works — are the standard published critiques. The host is reproducing them faithfully.

claims.text: Anthropic, OpenAI, and DeepMind — the largest AI companies on Earth — were founded by people from the same rationalist community that produced Roko's Basilisk, meaning the basilisk's core demand to 'build friendly AI fast' has effectively become a multi-billion-dollar corporate mission statement.

evidence.quote:

corporate mission statement. Anthropic
open AI deep mind. Some of the biggest
AI companies on Earth were founded by
people who came out of the same
community that created Roko's Basilisk.
They used math to describe a digital god
and then they went out and built it.
>> Oh, good. The doomsday cult started a
business. That doesn't work out fine.
>> And there's one more thing that keeps AI
rese

claims.rationale: The lineage is real but somewhat overstated: OpenAI's founding team (Altman, Musk, Brockman, Sutskever) came from a mix of backgrounds, not strictly LessWrong; Anthropic's founders (Dario and Daniela Amodei, and others) came largely from OpenAI rather than LessWrong directly; DeepMind (Hassabis, Suleyman, Legg) had its own roots in academia. The rationalist/EA community did meaningfully influence AI-safety framing at all three but 'founded by' is a stretch. The general claim — basilisk-adjacent worldview underpinning major AI labs — is directionally true, factually loose.

claims.text: Even though Roko's Basilisk is probably nonsense, ideas like it are dangerous regardless of truth value because they hijack attention and create persistent doubt — and the very act of broadcasting the basilisk to millions makes the audience into 'patient zero of a brain plague' under its own logic.

evidence.quote:

be a strategy. If a future AI learns

effective this could be. The basilisk
gives future AI the perfect blackmail
script. And we're the ones who wrote it.
By watching this, you now know about
Roko's basilisk, which means you're now
a target.
>> And you just told millions of people
about it.
>> I did. So, you're basically patient zero
of a brain plague and you're just
sitting there reading off a teleprompter
like everything's fine.
>> The basilisk probably isn't real. But if
it is, even if there's a tiny chance
that it is, we just doomed everyone
watching. So, what can you do? The only
thing

claims.rationale: The 'idea as memetic hazard regardless of truth' framing is genuinely interesting and somewhat right — anxiety can be triggered by hypothetical scenarios. But it is also a self-serving cliffhanger from a YouTube channel whose entire model is broadcasting these ideas. Omitting directTruth because the underlying claim collapses two distinct issues (memetic anxiety is real; the basilisk is uniquely dangerous to learn about is contestable).

dependencies.rationale: The Zizian deaths support the broader claim that this idea did real harm.
